rationale.R1.1: The section contains a direct contradiction in its contribution framing: paragraph 1 declares the multi-version index the 'central contribution,' while the Query Interface paragraph later overrides this, calling the scheduling policy the 'load-bearing contribution.' Resolving which claim the section actually argues for is foundational to any review.
rationale.R1.2: The dual-contribution contradiction is a structural logical failure. Additionally, the assertion that size-tiered compaction yields 'O(log N) tiers in expectation' conflates the number of tiers (bounded by a constant in size-tiered policy) with logarithmic scaling; this premise underlies the read-amplification tolerable-cost argument and must be checked.
rationale.R1.4: The section stakes novelty against Delta Lake (row- vs cell-granularity versioning), O'Neil et al. (LSM primitives as prior art vs. claimed extension), and Cassandra vs. RocksDB (compaction policy choice); accuracy of each claimed delta is a core review obligation.
rationale.R1.5: The narrative arc is inverted: the section leads with the storage index as the central contribution, inserts a Threat Model subsection, then pivots to declare the scheduler the actual load-bearing work—relegating the storage layer to scaffolding. This ordering obscures the section's argument and warrants structural assessment.
rationale.R1.7: Delta Lake's versioning granularity, Houlsby et al.'s BALD acquisition criterion, and Steinhardt et al.'s scope of adversarial annotation defenses are all cited substantively to justify design choices; attribution accuracy and the adequacy of coverage are live review responsibilities.
